title: Bell Pair Simulator
description: Simulate Bell-state creation and measurement in different bases to show the difference between entanglement and ordinary classical correlation.
whyItMatters: Bell states are the simplest usable entanglement resource. This project turns them from a symbol on a page into an operational object you can test.

steps.title: Prepare a Bell circuit
steps.description: Build a two-qubit circuit that creates one Bell state from a computational basis input.
steps.tips: Start with the textbook H plus CNOT construction.

steps.title: Measure in the computational basis
steps.description: Run repeated shots and confirm the strong correlations in the raw output.
steps.tips: Look at counts over many shots rather than individual runs.

steps.title: Rotate the basis
steps.description: Measure after basis-changing gates so the simulator reveals behaviour that a classical mixture would not reproduce in the same way.
steps.tips: This is where the physics becomes more than a probability table.

steps.title: Compare to a fake classical model
steps.description: Build a classical correlated baseline and show where it stops matching the quantum case.
steps.tips: The comparison is what makes the project educational.

steps.title: Write up the conclusion
steps.description: Explain what the project demonstrates about entanglement and why Bell states matter.
steps.tips: Keep the explanation precise and avoid science-fiction language.

deliverables: Measurement plots in at least two bases
Short comparison between quantum and classical correlations

rubric.description: The Bell-state preparation and measurements are implemented correctly.
The project clearly distinguishes entanglement from ordinary correlation.
The learner can explain the result without overclaiming what entanglement means.
